content.text: is the process by which an agent discovers, learns, and accumulates reusable, composable capabilities — called

— that can be invoked to solve future tasks more efficiently. A skill is an encapsulated unit of behavior: a named function, a code snippet, a prompt template, or a learned sub-policy that takes inputs and produces outputs. Rather than planning each task from raw LLM generation, a skill-equipped agent first checks its library for applicable capabilities and composes existing skills before synthesizing new solutions. The result is faster planning, more reliable execution, and generalization across structurally similar tasks.

attrs.alt: Skill acquisition architecture: Task Goal → Skill Planner → Skill Library → Skill Executor → Result, with a New Skill Learner path when the skill is absent, storing to Skill Store and indexing back into the library

content.text: Skill acquisition encompasses four related concepts from hierarchical reinforcement learning and agentic AI:

— identifying which capabilities to add to the library. Discovery methods include: imitation learning from demonstrations (observe how a human or expert agent solves a subtask and abstract the pattern), practice episodes (run many variations of a task and identify recurring sub-sequences), and
(ask an LLM to write a reusable function from examples).

— skills can themselves call other skills. Low-level skills (run_tests, read_file) are composed into mid-level skills (debug_module = run_tests + read_traceback + check_git_blame), which are composed into high-level skills (fix_regression). This hierarchy reduces planning depth dramatically.

— the formal RL abstraction for skills. Each option (skill) is a triple: an initiation set (when the skill is applicable), a policy (how to execute the skill), and a termination condition (when the skill ends). This gives a unified language for describing skill preconditions and effects.

— skills are described in natural language and embedded so the planner can search the library semantically. "Parse ISO timestamp" retrieves a stored skill even if the task description uses different words like "convert RFC 3339 date string".
Without skill acquisition, an agent must plan each task from scratch. This creates several problems at scale:
— a task with 20 atomic steps requires the planner to reason about all 20 at once. With skills, the same task might reduce to 4 skill invocations, collapsing the search space by 5×.
— a skill that is tested before being added to the library has a known success rate. The agent can rely on it; it doesn't need to re-derive the implementation each time.
— a skill learned on one task family ("parse structured output from LLM") transfers immediately to any other task that needs that capability.
— novel tasks can be solved by composing existing skills in new ways, even when the specific combination has never been seen before.
— with k skills the agent can solve exponentially more task varieties than k alone, because skills compose.
— skill-level planning uses far fewer LLM tokens than atomic-step planning. A high-level skill like "setup_dev_environment" encapsulates 15 steps in one invocation.
— skills tested before library entry are more reliable than freshly synthesized code. The agent benefits from prior validation.
— the library grows with every novel subtask, making the agent progressively more capable over its deployment lifetime.
— generating, testing, and embedding a new skill takes several LLM calls. For a task that occurs only once, this overhead is wasteful. Skills pay off when used ≥3 times.

— a skill synthesized for one codebase may hard-code assumptions (file paths, variable names, API endpoints) that fail in other contexts. Skill descriptions must generalize, and parameter interfaces must be flexible.
— with hundreds of skills, the agent faces a retrieval problem: which skill applies? Similar-sounding skills with different semantics cause planning errors. The library needs maintenance.
— deciding when an existing skill is "close enough" vs. when to synthesize a new one is non-trivial. Retrieving and adapting is cheaper; synthesizing from scratch is more correct for unusual tasks.
The formal RL foundation for skills is the
(Sutton, Precup & Singh, 1999). An option is defined as a triple:
— the states in which the option may be invoked,
— the sequence of actions the option takes, and
— the probability of ending the option at each state. In LLM agent terms:
maps to the skill's implementation (a function or tool sequence), and
— observe a human or oracle agent solving a task, identify recurring action sub-sequences, and abstract them into named functions. Works well for well-defined procedural skills.

— let the agent attempt many variations of a task family. After N runs, analyze which tool-call sequences appear repeatedly and package them as skills. The Voyager paper (Wang et al., 2023) used this approach in Minecraft: a code-writing LLM agent continually proposed and tested new skills.

— give the LLM a task description and ask it to write a reusable, parameterized Python function. Test the function, then add it to the library. This is on-demand skill creation: synthesize only when the library has no match.
Each skill is stored with a
(what it does and when to use it),
(implementation). The description is embedded for semantic retrieval. When the planner needs a capability, it embeds the task description and queries the skill store for the top-k matches.

The diagram above shows the skill acquisition loop. The Skill Planner decomposes the task into sub-goals and queries the Skill Library for each. Found skills go to the Executor. If no skill matches, the New Skill Learner generates, tests, and embeds a new skill before running it. The Skill Store persists the new entry and updates the vector index, making the skill available for future tasks.

— when the agent handles many instances of structurally similar tasks (debugging Python files, processing data pipelines, answering questions from a knowledge base), skills encoding the recurring sub-routines provide compounding value.
— when tasks decompose naturally into well-defined sub-tasks, those sub-tasks are natural skill boundaries. Use skill acquisition when you can articulate what "success" looks like for a sub-task.
— when the agent needs to operate across multiple codebases, APIs, or users where the high-level structure is similar but low-level details differ. Parameterized skills adapt to each context.
When planning latency is a concern
— skill-level planning generates far fewer tokens than atomic-step planning. If the planner's output must fit in one LLM call, compressing plans into skill invocations is essential.
A skill record captures everything needed to retrieve it and execute it:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any, Callable, Optional
import uuid

@dataclass
class Skill:
    id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    name: str = ""            # short identifier: "run_pytest"
    description: str = ""     # what it does + when to use it (this is embedded)
    signature: str = ""       # e.g., "run_pytest(test_file: str) -> dict"
    code: str = ""            # Python source of the implementation
    success_count: int = 0    # times used successfully
    failure_count: int = 0    # times it raised an exception
    version: int = 1
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def reliability(self) -> float:
        total = self.success_count + self.failure_count
        return self.success_count / total if total > 0 else 0.0

    def to_index_text(self) -> str:
        """Text embedded for retrieval search."""
        return f"{self.name}: {self.description}"
Example 1 — Using the Skill Library in an Agent Loop
The agent checks the library before synthesizing new code. If no skill matches, it generates and stores one:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
library = SkillLibrary()

def generate_skill(task_description: str) -> Skill:
    """
    Ask the LLM to write a reusable skill function for the given task.
    Returns an unvalidated Skill — caller must test before adding to library.
    """
    prompt = f"""Write a Python function to: {task_description}

Requirements:
- The function must be self-contained (import what it needs inside the function body).
- Use descriptive parameter names with type annotations.
- Return a typed result.
- Include a one-line docstring describing what the function does.
- Name the function using snake_case that describes its action.

Respond with ONLY the Python function code, no explanation."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=1000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    code = response.content[0].text.strip()
    # Extract function name from first line
    first_line = [l for l in code.split("\n") if l.startswith("def ")][0]
    func_name = first_line.split("(")[0].replace("def ", "").strip()

    return Skill(
        name=func_name,
        description=task_description,
        signature=first_line.strip().rstrip(":"),
        code=code,
    )

def agent_with_skills(task: str) -> str:
    """Agent that uses and learns skills."""
    # 1. Search the skill library
    matches = library.search(query=task, k=3)
    if matches:
        top_skill = matches[0]
        print(f"Using existing skill: {top_skill.name}")
        # Build prompt that uses the retrieved skill
        prompt = (
            f"You have access to this skill:\n\n"
            f"```python\n{top_skill.code}\n```\n\n"
            f"Use it to complete the task: {task}\n"
            f"Explain what you'll do and provide the invocation."
        )
    else:
        print("No matching skill found — synthesizing new skill.")
        new_skill = generate_skill(task)
        # Test it (simplified: just try to exec it without calling)
        try:
            compile(new_skill.code, "<skill>", "exec")
            library.add(new_skill)
            print(f"Added new skill: {new_skill.name}")
        except SyntaxError as e:
            print(f"Skill synthesis failed: {e}")
        prompt = f"Complete the task: {task}"

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=1500,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Seed the library with some initial skills
library.add(Skill(
    name="parse_iso_timestamp",
    description="Parse an ISO 8601 or RFC 3339 timestamp string into a Python datetime object",
    signature="parse_iso_timestamp(ts: str) -> datetime",
    code=(
        "from datetime import datetime\n"
        "import dateutil.parser\n\n"
        "def parse_iso_timestamp(ts: str):\n"
        "    \"\"\"Parse ISO 8601 / RFC 3339 timestamp into datetime.\"\"\"\n"
        "    return dateutil.parser.isoparse(ts)\n"
    ),
    tags=["datetime", "parsing"],
))

result = agent_with_skills("Convert an RFC 3339 date string to a Python datetime")
print(result)
Example 2 — Skill Discovery via LLM Code Generation
A more systematic discovery loop that processes task demonstrations and proposes skills:

def discover_skills_from_traces(
    traces: list[dict],
    library: SkillLibrary,
) -> list[Skill]:
    """
    Given a list of agent execution traces, ask the LLM to identify
    reusable sub-routines and propose skills.

    Each trace is: {task: str, steps: list[str], outcome: str}
    """
    traces_text = "\n\n".join(
        f"Task: {t['task']}\nSteps: {chr(10).join(t['steps'])}\nOutcome: {t['outcome']}"
        for t in traces
    )

    prompt = (
        "You are analyzing agent execution traces to extract reusable skills. "
        "For each recurring sub-routine you identify across the traces, propose a skill.\n\n"
        "Return a JSON array where each element has:\n"
        "- name: snake_case function name\n"
        "- description: one-sentence description of what it does and when to use it\n"
        "- code: complete Python function implementation\n\n"
        f"TRACES:\n{traces_text}\n\n"
        "Return only the JSON array, no explanation."
    )

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=2000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )

    import json, re
    text = response.content[0].text
    match = re.search(r'\[.*\]', text, re.DOTALL)
    if not match:
        return []
    proposals = json.loads(match.group())

    new_skills = []
    for p in proposals:
        skill = Skill(
            name=p["name"],
            description=p["description"],
            code=p["code"],
            signature=f"{p['name']}(...)",
        )
        try:
            compile(skill.code, "<skill>", "exec")  # syntax check
            library.add(skill)
            new_skills.append(skill)
            print(f"Discovered skill: {skill.name}")
        except SyntaxError:
            print(f"Skipped invalid skill: {skill.name}")
    return new_skills

# Example
traces = [
    {
        "task": "Debug failing pytest",
        "steps": ["Run pytest", "Parse traceback", "Check git blame on failing line"],
        "outcome": "Fixed import error",
    },
    {
        "task": "Fix type error",
        "steps": ["Run pytest", "Parse traceback", "Check git blame on failing line"],
        "outcome": "Added type cast",
    },
]
discovered = discover_skills_from_traces(traces, library)
# → Discovers: run_pytest, parse_traceback, git_blame_line
Real-World Example — Coding Agent Debugging Skills
A coding agent deployed to maintain a Python monorepo builds its debugging skill library over time:
Agent has no skills. Every debugging task requires full LLM-driven exploration: read all relevant files, run tests, analyze output, propose fix.
Library now has: run_pytest(file), extract_failing_tests(output), get_traceback(test_name), check_git_blame(file, line). Debugging tasks are 40% faster.
Skill composition: debug_import_error = run_pytest + extract_failing_tests + check_imports + fix_import. The planner calls one high-level skill instead of four atomic ones.
The agent handles 80% of bug tickets using skill composition alone, falling back to full LLM reasoning only for novel categories.
Treating every tool call as a new skill
— not every action deserves a library entry. Skills should be abstractions that recur and compose. A one-off action used once is not a skill.

— LLM-generated code frequently has bugs. An untested skill will fail at execution time, corrupting the agent's plan. Always compile-check and ideally test with a known input before adding to the library.

— a description like "run pytest for auth_service.py" is too narrow to retrieve when the agent needs to run tests for billing_service.py. Descriptions should describe the capability, not a specific invocation.

— run_pytest, execute_tests, run_test_suite, and pytest_runner are all the same capability. Without deduplication, the library fragments and retrieval quality drops. Run periodic deduplication using embedding clustering.
— when the underlying API changes, old skill implementations break. Track a version field and bump it on updates so agents can prefer newer versions.
— use verb_noun format: run_tests, parse_traceback, extract_entities. Consistent naming aids both retrieval and composition.
— skills should take the minimum required inputs and return typed results. A skill that takes 10 parameters is hard to invoke correctly. Prefer 2–3 clear parameters.
— maintain a test harness for the library. Each skill should have at least one example invocation. Run it before adding.
— maintain success_count and failure_count per skill. Retire skills with reliability < 0.6 after enough uses. Let reliability inform retrieval ranking.
Deduplication after each batch of additions
— after adding N new skills, cluster descriptions by embedding similarity and merge near-duplicates (cosine > 0.95) by keeping the higher-reliability version.

Skill libraries are typically small (100–2,000 skills). At this size, exact nearest-neighbor search completes in under 5 ms even without an ANN index. Only at 10,000+ skills does HNSW indexing become necessary. For most deployments, a simple FAISS flat index is sufficient.

Each skill search requires embedding the query string — one API call per agent step that needs a skill. This adds ~50 ms latency and a small cost (~$0.00002 per query). For high-throughput agents, batch queries or cache embeddings of the current task at session start.

— the Tool abstraction (name, description, function) is exactly a skill. LangChain's ToolNode and tool-calling agents implement skill selection and execution. Skills = tools with semantic search on top.

— the foundational paper and code for LLM-based skill acquisition in Minecraft. Agent iteratively proposes, tests, and stores skills in a growing library. Open-source at github.com/MineDreamer/Voyager.
— multi-agent framework with a built-in skill framework for actions (search, run_code, write_file) that agents can share and reuse.
— structured tool use APIs where the model selects and calls typed functions. This is the deployment-layer equivalent of skill invocation; the skill library maps to the set of available tools.
— benchmark and dataset for LLM-based tool use and skill selection across thousands of real-world APIs. Useful for evaluating skill retrieval quality.
Skills are code executed by the agent at runtime. LLM-generated code can contain bugs, system calls, network requests, or deliberately malicious instructions. Mitigations:
— run skills in a subprocess with restricted permissions, no network access, and a timeout. Use Python's
to check for dangerous patterns (os.system, subprocess, open with write) before adding a skill to the library. Reject skills that import disallowed modules.
Human review gate for sensitive skills
— for skills that touch the file system, databases, or external APIs, require a human to approve the skill before it enters the library.
A tiered validation approach balances safety and throughput: (1) syntax check via

(instant), (2) static analysis for dangerous patterns (fast), (3) unit test with known inputs (seconds), (4) human review for elevated-privilege skills (minutes). Only skills that pass all four stages for their risk level are promoted to the library.
Think of skill acquisition as building a

. A junior engineer solves every problem by reasoning from first principles. A senior engineer reaches for well-tested tools from their toolkit, and only builds new tools when none fit. The skill library IS that toolkit — not a replacement for intelligence, but an amplifier that lets intelligence operate at a higher level of abstraction. The agent doesn't stop reasoning; it reasons about
rather than reasoning about every atomic action.
A skill is a reusable, composable, named capability: code or a prompt template that encapsulates a recurring sub-task with defined inputs and outputs.

The options framework formalizes skills as (I, π, β) triples: initiation set (preconditions), policy (implementation), termination condition (postconditions). This unifies skill-based planning with classical RL.
Skills are discovered via imitation learning, practice episodes, or LLM program synthesis on demand. The latter is the most practical for LLM-based agents.
The skill library uses vector search over skill descriptions for semantic retrieval. Embed the description, not the code. The code is metadata.
Always test skills before adding to the library. Untested LLM-generated code will fail at execution time and corrupt the agent's plan.
Execute LLM-generated skill code in a sandbox. Apply static analysis to block dangerous imports (os.system, subprocess) before testing.
Skills compose hierarchically: low-level primitives build mid-level routines which build high-level behaviors. Each layer reduces planning complexity and improves reliability.
